content:
By Earthward Editorial Team · Earthward's crystal care and sourcing team · Updated 2026-06-28 · Reviewed by Earthward's mineral-sourcing reviewers

You picked up a crystal — maybe an amethyst point, a clear quartz cluster, a tumbled rose quartz — and a quiet question followed it home: is this actually real? It is a fair question, and more common than you might think. The crystal market is full of genuine stones, but it is also full of glass, plastic, dyed howlite, lab-grown quartz, and reconstituted material sold as natural. This guide walks through ten practical, mineralogy-based tests you can run at home, what each one can and cannot tell you, and how to read the marketplace signals that surround any purchase.

Quick Answer: How to Tell If a Crystal Is Real

Telling a real crystal from a fake comes down to three things: how the stone behaves physically (temperature, hardness, density, inclusions), whether its color and internal texture look natural, and whether the seller is transparent about what they are selling. No single test is conclusive, but a real crystal passes several of them at once — a fake tends to fail in clusters.

The three most reliable at-home tests to start with:

  • Temperature — a genuine crystal feels cool and stays cool, because minerals conduct heat better than glass or plastic. This is the fastest first check and harms nothing.
  • Bubbles under a 10x loupe — round bubbles mean glass; natural crystals show irregular inclusions (needles, veils) or none at all. A 10x jeweler's loupe costs about ten dollars and is the single best tool a buyer can own.
  • Price and naming — if a large "natural amethyst" geode costs the same as a tumbled stone, or the listing calls it a "purple crystal" instead of naming the mineral, those are signals worth investigating with the other tests.

No single home test is 100% conclusive — these tests work together. For valuable specimens, a gemological lab report is the gold standard.

Why Fake Crystals Are So Common

The fake-crystal market exists for a simple reason: natural specimens are finite, mining and cutting them costs real money, and demand has grown faster than ethical supply. Glass, plastic, and lab-grown material are cheap to produce at scale. A factory can turn out a thousand identical "amethyst" beads from purple glass for the cost of cutting a single genuine stone. When the gap between the real price and the fake price is large enough, the economic pressure to substitute is enormous — and the further a buyer is from the source, the easier substitution becomes.

Fakes fall into a few recognizable categories. Glass imitations are the most common — dyed to mimic amethyst, citrine, or rose quartz, and often sold under vague names. Plastic and resin fakes show up in cheap beaded bracelets, especially for opaque stones like turquoise and malachite. Dyed stones substitute a cheap mineral for an expensive one: howlite dyed blue to imitate turquoise, or jasper dyed to look like lapis. Lab-grown (synthetic) quartz is chemically identical to natural quartz but is created in a factory — legitimate if disclosed, misleading if sold as natural. Reconstituted material — ground quartz melted with glass — occupies a gray zone. And then there is the naming trap: the glass industry calls leaded glass "crystal," which has nothing to do with natural mineral crystals.

Fakes are hard to spot because the best imitations copy the surface — the color, the shape, the shine. They do not copy the internal structure, because that internal structure is exactly what makes a natural crystal natural: the ordered atomic lattice, the slow-grown inclusions, the species-specific density and hardness. So the reliable tests look past the surface. They ask how the stone conducts heat, what is trapped inside it, whether it scratches glass, and how heavy it feels. A buyer who learns a handful of these tests can read a stone the way a jeweler does — not perfectly, but well enough to avoid the obvious fakes.

How to Tell If a Crystal Is Real: 10 Tests

Below are ten tests, ordered roughly from easiest and least destructive to most confirmatory. Most use only your senses, a glass of water, or a $10 loupe. The scratch test is the only destructive one — use it carefully, and only on rough or tumbled quartz-family stones, never on finished jewelry. Each test explains the science behind it, what a genuine stone versus a fake looks like, how to do it, and — importantly — where the test breaks down.

🌡️ Test 1: Temperature Test

The science behind it: Minerals transfer heat faster than glass or plastic because their ordered crystalline lattice conducts thermal energy more efficiently than the disordered amorphous structure of glass. A natural crystal at room temperature (around 20°C) therefore feels noticeably cool when first touched and stays cool longer, while glass and plastic — which conduct heat poorly — quickly warm to skin temperature.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Initial touchNoticeably coolCloser to room temperature
After 10 seconds in handStill coolWarmed up
Thermal conductivityHigh (crystal lattice)Low (amorphous glass / polymer)

How to do it:

  1. Hold the stone in your palm or against your inner wrist (more temperature-sensitive than fingertips) for about 10 seconds.
  2. Compare the feel against a known glass object of similar size (a glass bead or marble) at the same room temperature.
  3. A genuine quartz, amethyst, or citrine keeps a cool edge; glass warms quickly.

Limitations: Room temperature, body heat, and ambient humidity all affect the result — test in a neutral 20°C room, not after the stone sat in sunlight or a hot car. Small beads (under 8mm) are harder to judge by touch because they warm up faster regardless of material. This is a useful first signal, never a standalone verdict.

Best stones for this test: Quartz family (clear quartz, amethyst, citrine, rose quartz, smoky quartz), topaz. Less reliable for opaque stones (jasper, howlite) where surface texture masks the temperature cue.

Source: mindat.org — mineral thermal conductivity; GIA Gem Reference Guide — quartz identification.

🔨 Test 2: Hardness / Scratch Test

The science behind it: The Mohs scale ranks minerals by scratch resistance from 1 (talc) to 10 (diamond). Genuine quartz sits at Mohs 7, meaning it can scratch ordinary window glass (about Mohs 5.5) and steel (around Mohs 6). Glass imitations, being softer or equally soft, cannot reliably scratch true glass. The test exploits this hardness gap.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Scratching glassQuartz (Mohs 7) scratches glass easilyGlass (Mohs 5.5) cannot scratch glass
Being scratched by steelSteel (Mohs 6) cannot scratch quartzSteel can scratch glass imitation
Edge after scratchLeaves a clear grooveNo mark, or only crumbles

How to do it:

  1. Take a piece of ordinary window glass (a glass jar you do not mind damaging, never good glassware).
  2. Press a sharp edge or point of the stone firmly and drag it across the glass.
  3. A genuine quartz leaves a visible scratch groove; a glass imitation leaves no mark or only a faint streak that rubs off.

Limitations: This is a destructive test — it damages both the stone (edge) and the glass. Never use it on finished jewelry, polished pieces, or stones you want to keep pristine. It does not work for genuine soft stones (malachite Mohs 3.5-4, calcite Mohs 3, turquoise Mohs 5-6) which legitimately cannot scratch glass. Use only as a confirmation on quartz-family rough or tumbled stones.

Best stones for this test: Quartz family (Mohs 7), topaz (Mohs 8), jadeite (Mohs 6.5-7). Not suitable for soft stones, jewelry, or polished specimens.

Source: mindat.org — Mohs hardness scale; USGS Mineral Database — quartz hardness.

⚖️ Test 3: Specific Gravity (Heft) Test

The science behind it: Specific gravity (SG) measures density relative to water. Natural quartz has an SG of 2.65 — meaning a given volume of quartz weighs 2.65 times the same volume of water. Most common glass falls around 2.4-2.6, plastic around 1.0-1.5. Holding two stones of the same size, the denser genuine crystal feels noticeably heavier in the hand ("good heft"), while plastic feels suspiciously light.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Weight in hand for same sizeSubstantial — "good heft" (SG 2.65 quartz)Lighter (glass ~2.5, plastic ~1.2)
Ratio to water displacement2.65 (quartz), 3.3 (jadeite)2.4-2.6 (glass), 1.0-1.5 (plastic)
Tapping two same-size beadsDenser, deeper tapHollower tap

How to do it:

  1. Compare the suspected stone against a known genuine stone of similar size (or against a glass marble of the same diameter).
  2. Weigh them in your palm alternately — a genuine quartz bead feels about 10% heavier than an equal-size glass bead.
  3. For precision: weigh the stone dry, then suspended in water, and divide — but for most home checks, hand heft is enough.

Limitations: The difference between quartz (2.65) and glass (2.4-2.6) is small enough that hand heft alone is unreliable without a reference object of the same size. Plastic (1.0-1.5) is obvious, but high-quality glass imitations can feel similar. A precision jewelry scale with water displacement is needed for an accurate SG reading.

Best stones for this test: All stones where there is a meaningful density gap — especially distinguishing plastic imitations of any stone, and jadeite (SG 3.3, distinctly heavier than the quartz or serpentine often substituted).

Source: mindat.org — mineral specific gravity data; GIA Gem Reference Guide.

🔍 Test 4: Bubble Inspection (10x Loupe)

The science behind it: Manufactured glass traps small spherical or teardrop-shaped air bubbles as it cools — a tell-tale sign of industrial origin. Natural minerals form over geological time and instead contain natural inclusions: needle-like rutile, two-phase gas-liquid inclusions, mineral crystals, or "fingerprints" of healed fractures. A 10x jeweler's loupe reveals the difference: round bubbles mean glass; irregular inclusions mean natural.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Under 10x loupeNeedle/plate inclusions, growth lines, mineral flecksPerfectly round bubbles, swirls, no irregular inclusions
Bubble shapeNone (natural minerals rarely trap air)Spherical or teardrop bubbles
Internal textureChaotic, asymmetric, "natural"Too clean, or with flow lines from manufacturing

How to do it:

  1. Use a 10x jeweler's loupe (an inexpensive tool — about $10-15 online).
  2. Hold the stone against a light source (window or lamp) and look through it with the loupe.
  3. Scan for perfectly round, spherical bubbles — these are characteristic of glass. Natural stones show irregular inclusions instead.

Limitations: High-quality optical glass and fused quartz can be manufactured bubble-free, so the absence of bubbles does not guarantee natural origin — but the presence of round bubbles almost always indicates glass. Some natural stones (like enhydro quartz) do contain moving water bubbles, but these are elongated or angular, not perfectly round. Requires a loupe; the naked eye usually cannot resolve the smallest bubbles.

Best stones for this test: Transparent stones sold as clear quartz, citrine, topaz, or diamond simulants. Less useful for opaque stones where you cannot see inside.

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — inclusions in natural vs. synthetic; mindat.org.

🌈 Test 5: Color Banding & Zoning

The science behind it: Natural color in minerals forms unevenly because crystal growth happens over geological time under changing conditions. Genuine amethyst shows subtle color zoning — deeper purple at the tips, lighter near the base, with natural gradation. Synthetic quartz grown in a lab colors uniformly and looks "flat" or overly saturated. Dyed stones concentrate pigment in surface cracks and fissures rather than throughout.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Color distributionUneven zoning, natural gradationUniform, flat, "too perfect"
Where color sitsThroughout the crystal, following growth structureDyed: concentrated in cracks/fissures; synthetic: perfectly even
SaturationSubtle variation, lighter and darker zonesSingle flat tone, often over-saturated

How to do it:

  1. Look at the stone under daylight (not yellow indoor light, which distorts color).
  2. Examine whether the color is uniform or shows natural lighter/darker zones — genuine amethyst, citrine, and fluorite almost always show some variation.
  3. For suspected dye: check cracks and chips — dye pools in surface fissures and looks more intense there.

Limitations: Some high-quality natural stones are genuinely uniform in color, and some synthetic stones are deliberately made with simulated zoning. So uniform color is a flag, not a verdict — combine with other tests. Dye tests can fade over time; a cotton swab dampened with acetone (for non-quartz stones) can lift surface dye, but this is mildly destructive.

Best stones for this test: Amethyst (most classic case), citrine, fluorite, agate, rose quartz, lapis lazuli. Not useful for naturally uniform stones or opaque dyed stones without cracks to inspect.

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — quartz color zoning; mindat.org — amethyst color cause (iron + irradiation).

🟣 Test 6: UV Light (Fluorescence) Test

The science behind it: Some minerals fluoresce — emit visible light — under ultraviolet (UV) radiation, due to trace impurities called activators. Natural fluorite often fluoresces blue or purple; genuine scapolite glows yellow-orange; some natural quartz shows weak fluorescence. The fluorescence pattern of a natural stone differs from glass (usually inert) and from some synthetics (which may fluoresce differently). A 365nm UV flashlight reveals this.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Under 365nm UV lightSpecific fluorescence (e.g., fluorite → blue/purple)Glass usually inert; some synthetics fluoresce abnormally
Fluorescence patternPatchy, follows natural growth zonesUniform or absent (glass), or too uniform (some synthetics)
Color of glowSpecies-specific (scapolite yellow, fluorite blue)None, or wrong color for the claimed species

How to do it:

  1. Use a 365nm UV flashlight (about $10-15 online; the shorter 395nm "blacklight" is less precise).
  2. Darken the room as much as possible.
  3. Shine the UV on the stone and observe any glow — note the color and whether it is patchy or uniform.

Limitations: Not all minerals fluoresce — many genuine stones (including most clear quartz) show no reaction, so a negative result does not mean fake. Fluorescence also varies even within a single mineral species depending on impurities. This test is most useful as confirmation for known fluorescent species (fluorite, scapolite, some calcite, some amber). Requires a UV light source and a dark room.

Best stones for this test: Fluorite (reliable blue/purple), scapolite, amber (fluoresces blue-green), some calcite. Not useful as a general test for non-fluorescent species like clear quartz.

Source: mindat.org — mineral fluorescence data; GIA Gem Reference Guide.

💎 Test 7: Refractive Index (RI) Test

The science behind it: When light enters a transparent material, it bends at a characteristic angle — the refractive index (RI). Quartz has an RI of 1.544, common glass around 1.50-1.52, diamond 2.417. A gemological refractometer measures this precisely. The gap between quartz (1.544) and glass (1.50) is small but measurable; diamond and its simulants differ dramatically.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Refractive index readingQuartz: 1.544 (precise)Glass: 1.50-1.52 (lower)
Birefringence (double refraction)Quartz shows slight doubling (0.009)Glass is singly refractive (no doubling)
Reading stabilitySharp, single species-specific readingVariable or off-spec for the claimed stone

How to do it:

  1. This requires a gemological refractometer (a professional instrument, $100-300 for a basic model) and contact fluid (refractive index liquid).
  2. Place a polished flat face of the stone on the refractometer prism with a drop of contact fluid.
  3. Look through the eyepiece and read the boundary line — compare against known values for the claimed species.

Limitations: Refractometers are expensive and require practice to read. The RI difference between quartz (1.544) and glass (1.50-1.52) is small enough that home use is difficult without experience. Many natural stones share overlapping RI ranges, so RI alone rarely gives a unique identification — it confirms or rules out rather than definitively names.

Best stones for this test: Distinguishing diamond (RI 2.417) from simulants, separating quartz from glass in valuable specimens, identifying ruby/sapphire vs. glass. Not a practical home test for inexpensive stones.

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — refractive index tables; mindat.org.

🔔 Test 8: Sound / Tap Test

The science behind it: A genuine crystal has an ordered internal structure that resonates when struck — producing a clear, sustained ring or chime. Glass, with its disordered amorphous structure, produces a shorter, duller "clink." This is the same principle that distinguishes crystal wine glasses (lead crystal, structured) from ordinary glass.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Sound when tappedClear, sustained ring / chimeShort, dull clink
Resonance durationLingersDies quickly
PitchHigher, musicalLower, flat

How to do it:

  1. Suspend the stone on a string, or hold it lightly by the edge (not gripped, which dampens sound).
  2. Tap it gently with another hard object — a metal spoon, a glass rod, or another crystal.
  3. Listen: a genuine quartz crystal point produces a clear ring; glass produces a dull clink.

Limitations: Highly subjective — requires a reference for comparison and some ear training. Small tumbled stones and beads produce little resonance regardless of material, making this test unreliable for jewelry. Background noise interferes. Most useful on larger crystal points and clusters, less so on small pieces.

Best stones for this test: Larger clear quartz points and clusters, singing bowls, large crystals. Not useful for small beads, tumbled stones, or jewelry.

Source: mineralogy reference — acoustic resonance of crystalline vs. amorphous solids.

💰 Test 9: Price Red Flag

The science behind it: Genuine natural crystals carry real costs: mining, sorting, cutting, polishing, transport, and the reality that high-quality specimens are finite. A price far below the established market range signals that something is off — either synthetic, glass, dyed, or reconstituted material being sold as natural. While promotions exist, a consistent dramatic discount across a seller's entire catalog is a structural red flag, not a sale.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Price vs. market rangeWithin reasonable market range for the species and qualityFar below market (often 50-80% cheaper)
Consistency of discountOccasional promotions on specific piecesEntire catalog suspiciously cheap
Quality claims vs. pricePrice matches claimed grade"Museum quality" at bargain prices

How to do it:

  1. Compare the seller's prices against established reference points (gemological market guides, reputable dealers).
  2. Be wary of "too good to be true" pricing — a large natural amethyst geode for the price of a tumbled stone is not a deal, it is a different material.
  3. Use price as one signal among many — a fair price does not guarantee authenticity, but a wildly low price is a strong warning.

Limitations: Price alone never confirms authenticity — a seller can charge full price for glass. And genuine inexpensive stones exist (clear quartz, common agate, rose quartz rough are legitimately affordable). Use price as an auxiliary signal: a red flag warrants deeper testing, but a reasonable price does not exempt a stone from the other tests.

Best stones for this test: All stones, as an auxiliary signal — especially high-value species (jadeite, moldavite, large amethyst geodes, tanzanite) where prices are well-established and dramatic discounts are implausible.

Source: market reference — gemological pricing guides.

🏷️ Test 10: Naming & Source Transparency

The science behind it: Reputable sellers identify stones by their proper mineral name and origin, because mineral species and locality affect value and care. Vague or misleading names are a marketing red flag: the glass industry calls leaded glass "crystal" (lead crystal, crystal glass), so "crystal quartz" can mean glass rather than natural quartz. Sellers who hide behind "mystery stone," "energy crystal," or omit locality are harder to hold accountable.

Real vs fake at a glance:

SignalGenuineFake (glass / plastic)
Stone name on listingProper mineral name (Natural Amethyst, SiO₂)Vague ("purple crystal," "energy stone") or industry code ("crystal quartz" = glass)
Treatment disclosureDisclosed (heat-treated citrine, lab-created)Hidden or denied
LocalitySpecific origin (Brazil, Uruguay, Zambia)Generic ("imported," none listed)

How to do it:

  1. Read the listing carefully — does it name the actual mineral species, or use vague marketing terms?
  2. Check for treatment disclosure: reputable sellers note heat treatment, irradiation, dyeing, or lab creation.
  3. Look for locality information — specific origins (Brazil for amethyst, Madagascar for labradorite) signal a seller who knows their stock.

Limitations: A seller who names stones correctly can still sell glass; and a small honest seller may omit locality without ill intent. Naming transparency is a positive signal but not proof. Conversely, vague naming is a warning, not a verdict. Use it to decide which sellers warrant deeper trust, combined with the other tests.

Best stones for this test: All stones, especially when buying online sight-unseen. The single most useful pre-purchase signal.

Source: GIA / FTC guidelines on gemstone disclosure.

Crystal vs Glass: What's the Difference?

Here is the single biggest source of confusion in the whole topic. The glass and tableware industry uses the word "crystal" to mean leaded glass — "lead crystal," "crystal glass," the material in wine glasses and chandeliers. That usage is centuries old and perfectly legal in its own industry. But natural mineral crystals — quartz, amethyst, citrine — are something completely different: they are minerals with an ordered internal atomic structure, formed over geological time. When a listing says "crystal quartz" at a suspiciously low price, it often means glass, not natural quartz. The naming overlap is not a conspiracy; it is a historical accident that buyers have to navigate.

At a chemical level, natural quartz is silicon dioxide (SiO₂) — a crystalline mineral with a regular, repeating atomic lattice. Glass, including lead crystal glassware, is an amorphous solid: its atoms are arranged in a disordered, frozen-liquid structure, typically with sodium, calcium, or lead mixed in. That structural difference is the whole reason the tests work. The ordered lattice of quartz conducts heat faster, scratches harder, and bends light at a specific angle (refractive index 1.544) — none of which the disordered structure of glass replicates exactly.

Five quick checks separate crystal from glass. Temperature: quartz feels cool and stays cool; glass warms. Hardness: quartz (Mohs 7) scratches ordinary glass; glass cannot scratch glass. Bubbles under a loupe: glass traps round air bubbles; natural quartz shows irregular inclusions or none. Refractive index: quartz bends light at 1.544, glass at roughly 1.50 — measurable with a refractometer. Sound: a quartz point rings clear when tapped; glass gives a dull clink. Run any two of these and the picture gets clear quickly.

Glass impersonates crystal for three reasons: it looks similar (transparent, shiny), it costs a fraction as much to produce, and it takes dye easily. For a seller moving volume, glass beads labeled as "natural crystal" carry a margin that genuine stones cannot match. This is why the tests below matter — not to catch every sophisticated forgery, but to filter out the bulk of the market where glass is sold as crystal. Once you can recognize the pattern, the obvious fakes stand out.

Mineral Data Reference (Mohs Hardness, Specific Gravity, Refractive Index)

The numbers behind the tests — these are fixed mineralogical facts, not opinion. Use them to verify what the home tests suggest.

StoneMohs hardnessSpecific gravityRefractive indexFormulaCommon imitation
Amethyst72.651.544SiO₂ (silicon dioxide)Synthetic (lab-grown) purple quartz
Clear Quartz72.651.544SiO₂ (silicon dioxide)Ordinary glass (most common)
Turquoise5–62.6–2.9~1.62CuAl₆(PO₄)₄(OH)₈·4H₂O (hydrated copper aluminum phosphate)Howlite dyed blue (the most common fake)
Lapis Lazuli5–5.52.7–2.9~1.50Lazurite + calcite + pyrite (a rock, not a single mineral)Dyed jasper or howlite

Mineral data: GIA Gem Reference Guide, mindat.org, USGS Mineral Database. SG = specific gravity; RI = refractive index.

Stone-Specific Tests: The Most Faked Crystals

Some stones are faked far more often than others. The four below — amethyst, clear quartz, turquoise, and lapis lazuli — cover the great majority of fakes a buyer will encounter. Each has a single headline test that catches most imitations. (For amethyst and clear quartz we have dedicated deep-dive guides; the overviews here hit the essentials.)

Amethyst

Mineral data: Formula SiO₂ (silicon dioxide) · Mohs 7 · Specific gravity 2.65 · Refractive index 1.544 · Color cause: Iron + natural irradiation. Primary origins: Brazil, Uruguay, Zambia, Mexico.

What gets faked:

  • Synthetic (lab-grown) purple quartz
  • Glass dyed purple
  • Heat-treated low-grade amethyst sold as "citrine"

How to tell Amethyst is real — the key tests:

  1. Color zoning is the headline test — natural amethyst shows subtle lighter/darker purple gradients, especially concentrated at crystal tips. Synthetic amethyst looks uniformly saturated and "too perfect."
  2. Inclusions under 10x loupe: natural amethyst has needle-like rutile, two-phase gas-liquid inclusions, or "fingerprints." Synthetic amethyst often shows "breadcrumb" inclusions from lab seed-crystal growth.
  3. UV fluorescence: synthetic amethyst frequently shows a blue fluorescence under shortwave UV; natural amethyst is usually inert or shows weak reddish-brown.
  4. Pricing sanity check: a large "natural amethyst" geode at the price of a tumbled stone is almost certainly dyed or reconstituted.

Price sanity check: Tumbled stones $2-8; beads $0.50-3 each; small geodes $15-60; large cathedral geodes $100-2000+ depending on size and quality

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — quartz; mindat.org — amethyst (Fe³⁺ + irradiation color cause).

Read the full Amethyst meaning · Shop Amethyst

Clear Quartz

Mineral data: Formula SiO₂ (silicon dioxide) · Mohs 7 · Specific gravity 2.65 · Refractive index 1.544 · Color cause: Colorless (no impurities). Primary origins: Brazil, Madagascar, Arkansas (USA), Himalayas.

What gets faked:

  • Ordinary glass (most common)
  • Reconstituted quartz (quartz powder melted with glass)
  • Synthetic lab-grown quartz
  • Lead crystal glass ("crystal" naming trap)

How to tell Clear Quartz is real — the key tests:

  1. Bubbles under 10x loupe are the #1 signal — glass has round air bubbles; genuine clear quartz has irregular inclusions (needles, veils, mineral flecks) or is inclusion-free.
  2. Hardness test: genuine clear quartz (Mohs 7) scratches glass; glass imitations cannot.
  3. The "crystal quartz" naming trap — the glass industry calls leaded glass "crystal" (lead crystal, crystal glass). "Crystal quartz" sold cheaply is often glass. Natural quartz is properly called "quartz."
  4. Three quality tiers to distinguish: natural clear quartz (has minor inclusions), clarified/reconstituted (too perfect, may show faint flow lines), and pure glass (bubbles, no quartz characteristics).
  5. Double refraction: genuine quartz shows slight doubling when you look through it at a sharp angle (text appears doubled); glass does not — but this needs magnification to see.

Price sanity check: Small tumbled $1-4; small points $3-10; clusters $10-50; large cathedral points $30-300; museum-grade clusters $200-2000+

Source: mindat.org — quartz; GIA Gem Reference Guide — quartz RI 1.544, birefringence 0.009.

Read the full Clear Quartz meaning · Shop Clear Quartz

Turquoise

Mineral data: Formula CuAl₆(PO₄)₄(OH)₈·4H₂O (hydrated copper aluminum phosphate) · Mohs 5–6 · Specific gravity 2.6–2.9 · Refractive index ~1.62 · Color cause: Copper (blue), iron (green). Primary origins: Iran, USA (Southwest), China, Mexico, Egypt.

What gets faked:

  • Howlite dyed blue (the most common fake)
  • Plastic / resin (block turquoise, "reconstituted")
  • Magnesite dyed
  • Glass

How to tell Turquoise is real — the key tests:

  1. Matrix inspection: genuine turquoise has a natural matrix (host rock veins — brown, black, or golden). Dyed howlite has dark veining that looks "spiderweb" but the base color is wrong (too uniform blue, matrix too regular).
  2. The "too perfect" test: genuine turquoise varies in color across a piece; dyed howlite and plastic are uniform.
  3. Specific gravity: plastic block turquoise feels suspiciously light; genuine turquoise has a heftier feel.
  4. Price flag: large "natural turquoise" beads at $1-2 each are almost certainly howlite or plastic.
  5. Stabilization disclosure: most genuine turquoise on the market is "stabilized" (resin-impregnated for durability) — reputable sellers disclose this; it is legitimate, but should not be sold as "untreated."

Price sanity check: Genuine tumbled $5-20; beads $1-5; untreated Sleeping Beauty grade $20-100+; fakes often $1-3

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — turquoise; mindat.org — turquoise formula and origins.

Read the full Turquoise meaning · Shop Turquoise

Lapis Lazuli

Mineral data: Formula Lazurite + calcite + pyrite (a rock, not a single mineral) · Mohs 5–5.5 · Specific gravity 2.7–2.9 · Refractive index ~1.50 · Color cause: Lazurite sulfur-bearing radicals (blue), pyrite (gold flecks). Primary origins: Afghanistan (primary, finest), Chile, Russia, Pakistan.

What gets faked:

  • Dyed jasper or howlite
  • Synthetic lapis (Gilson)
  • Sodalite sold as lapis
  • Dyed marble

How to tell Lapis Lazuli is real — the key tests:

  1. Pyrite flecks: genuine lapis has real gold-colored pyrite flecks scattered irregularly — they look metallic and catch light. Fakes either have no pyrite, or pyrite painted/printed in too-uniform dots.
  2. Calcite white veining: genuine lapis often shows white calcite streaks. Perfectly uniform deep-blue lapis with no calcite or pyrite is suspicious.
  3. The "too blue" test: dyed howlite and jasper achieve an unnaturally uniform royal blue; genuine lapis has natural variation.
  4. Sodalite substitution: sodalite is a related blue mineral but lacks pyrite and has a different (often more violet) tone — sold cheaply as "lapis."

Price sanity check: Tumbled $3-12; beads $0.50-4; high-grade Afghan with pyrite $10-50 per piece

Source: GIA Gem Reference Guide — lapis lazuli; mindat.org — lazurite.

When to Use Professional Tools

Most home tests need nothing more than your hands, your eyes, and a glass of water. A few inexpensive tools raise your confidence considerably, and for valuable specimens, only a professional lab can give a definitive answer.

  • A 10x jeweler's loupe (about $10-15) is the highest-value tool a buyer can own. It reveals bubbles in glass, inclusions in natural stones, dye concentrations in cracks, and the difference between natural growth texture and manufactured perfection. Every test in the "bubbles" and "color banding" sections depends on it.
  • A small Mohs hardness kit (a set of picks of known hardness, $15-30) lets you test scratch resistance precisely without guessing at a steel edge. Useful for identifying quartz-family stones and distinguishing them from softer look-alikes. Remember: scratch testing is destructive — use only on rough or stones you do not mind marking.
  • A 365nm UV flashlight ($10-15) reveals fluorescence in minerals that react — fluorite (blue/purple), scapolite (yellow-orange), some amber. It is a confirmation test for known fluorescent species, not a general "fake detector," since many genuine stones show no reaction at all.
  • A gemological refractometer ($100-300 for a basic model) measures refractive index precisely. This is a professional-grade tool — most buyers will not own one, but it is what a gemologist uses to separate quartz (1.544) from glass (1.50) or diamond (2.417) from simulants in a measurable, repeatable way.
  • For high-value specimens — jadeite bangles, imperial-grade amethyst, large moldavite, untreated turquoise, or any piece priced above a few hundred dollars — a gemological laboratory certificate is the only definitive answer. Labs like GIA, IGI, or AGS issue reports that confirm species, treatments, and origin. Home tests narrow the odds; a lab report settles them.

For high-value specimens (jadeite, imperial-grade amethyst, moldavite, large geodes), a gemological laboratory certificate is the only definitive answer — home tests narrow the odds, they do not replace a professional report.

Frequently Asked Questions About Real vs Fake Crystal

How can you tell if a crystal is real or fake?

Run several tests together, because a real crystal passes them in clusters while a fake fails in clusters. The fastest checks: does it feel cool and stay cool (temperature), does it have round bubbles under a 10x loupe (glass does, natural crystals do not), and does it scratch ordinary glass if it is a quartz-family stone. Add the marketplace signals — is the price reasonable for the species, and does the seller name the actual mineral and its origin? No single home test is 100% conclusive, but a genuine stone that passes four or five of these tests is very likely real, and a stone that fails two or three is very likely not.

What is the most reliable test for real crystals?

The temperature test combined with the bubble inspection is the most reliable starting pair, because both are non-destructive and quick. A genuine quartz-family stone feels cool and stays cool in your hand, and under a 10x loupe it shows irregular inclusions or is inclusion-free — never round bubbles. For opaque stones like turquoise and malachite, the specific gravity (heft) test and careful inspection of color variation are more reliable than the bubble test, since you cannot see inside an opaque stone.

Can you tell if a crystal is real at home?

Yes — most of the useful tests work at home with no special equipment. Temperature, visual color inspection, the bubble check (with a $10 loupe), the heft comparison against a known stone, and the price-and-naming read all happen at your kitchen table. The only home test that needs caution is the scratch (hardness) test, which is destructive — use it only on rough or tumbled quartz-family stones, never on jewelry you want to keep pristine. For valuable specimens, a gemological lab is the next step beyond what home testing can settle.

Is there a difference between crystal and glass?

Yes, and the confusion is built into the language. In the glass and tableware industry, "crystal" means leaded glass — the material in wine glasses and chandeliers, which is an amorphous glass with no crystalline structure. In mineralogy, a "crystal" is a solid with an ordered, repeating atomic lattice — like quartz, amethyst, or fluorite. When a crystal seller says "crystal," they mean the mineral. When a glassware maker says "crystal," they mean lead glass. The two materials behave differently: mineral crystals conduct heat faster, scratch harder, and bend light at specific angles, while glass does none of those things exactly. If a listing says "crystal quartz" at a surprisingly low price, read carefully — it may be glass using the tableware convention.

Where can I buy genuine crystals?

Look for sellers who do three things: name each stone by its actual mineral species (Natural Amethyst, SiO₂ — not vague terms like "purple crystal"), disclose any treatments (heat-treated, dyed, lab-created, stabilized), and state a specific origin (Brazil, Uruguay, Afghanistan). Beyond that, check whether the seller provides mineral data (Mohs hardness, specific gravity), offers a clear return policy, and uses real photographs that show natural variation between pieces rather than identical catalog shots. Sellers who do all of this are easier to hold accountable and tend to know their stock. The crystal series on this site names stones and sources for exactly this reason.

How much should a real crystal cost?

Prices vary widely by species, quality, and size, so there is no single "crystal price." A few reference points: tumbled clear quartz and rose quartz run $2-6; small amethyst points $5-15; a small genuine turquoise tumble $5-20; moldavite has risen sharply and starts around $20-80 per gram for genuine pieces. The useful question is not the absolute price but the ratio to the species — a large "natural amethyst" geode at the price of a tumbled stone, or a big "moldavite" piece for ten dollars, is a red flag regardless of the seller. Learn the rough market range for the species you want, and treat prices far below it as a reason to apply the other tests carefully.

Are lab-created crystals real?

Lab-created (synthetic) crystals are real in the sense that they share the same chemical composition and crystal structure as natural stones — synthetic amethyst is genuinely SiO₂ with the same hardness and density. What they are not is natural: they were grown in a factory rather than mined, usually over weeks rather than geological time. The issue is disclosure, not the material itself. Selling synthetic quartz as natural, without disclosure, is misleading and against FTC guidelines. Reputable sellers label lab-created stones clearly. If you want a natural specimen — for the inclusions, the growth history, or the symbolic connection to geological time — ask explicitly and read the listing.

Can home tests guarantee a crystal is real?

No — and any guide that promises a 100% conclusive home test is overpromising. Home tests are excellent at filtering out the obvious fakes: glass sold as quartz, plastic sold as turquoise, dyed howlite sold as lapis. They narrow the odds considerably. But sophisticated imitations — high-quality synthetic quartz, well-made reconstituted material, expertly dyed stones — can pass several home tests. For a specimen of real value, the only definitive answer is a report from a gemological laboratory (GIA, IGI, AGS), which uses refractometry, spectroscopy, and microscopic inclusion analysis that no home setup can match. Think of home tests as a strong first filter, not a final verdict.

Are fake crystals dangerous?

Most fake crystals are not dangerous in a health sense — they are glass, plastic, or dyed stone, which are inert materials. The real cost is financial and emotional: paying for something you did not receive. There are a few mineral-specific notes worth knowing. Malachite is copper-bearing, so "malachite water" (soaking the stone and drinking the water) is not safe — copper compounds are water-soluble. Dyed stones can bleed pigment onto skin or fabric, especially cheap dyed howlite or agate. Beyond these specific cases, the concern with fakes is honesty, not toxicity. A glass bead sold as amethyst will not hurt you, but it is not what you were told it was.

What does 'crystal' mean in glassware?

Historical accident. The word "crystal" comes from the Greek "krystallos," meaning ice — ancient writers thought clear quartz was permanently frozen ice. When Venetian glassmakers in the 1600s added lead to glass to make it sparkle like the natural quartz they admired, they borrowed the name: "lead crystal," "crystal glass." The usage stuck in the tableware industry. So today "crystal" means two unrelated things depending on context — a natural mineral with an ordered lattice, or a type of leaded glass. Crystal sellers mean the mineral; glassware makers mean the glass. When a price seems too good, check which meaning is in play.

Why does my crystal feel warm?

Most likely because your hand is warm and the stone is small. A genuine crystal does feel cool at first touch, because minerals conduct heat away from your skin faster than body temperature. But any small object — glass, plastic, even a real crystal bead under 8mm — warms up in a closed hand within a minute, because there is not enough mass to keep drawing heat. The temperature test works best on larger pieces (a palm-sized cluster, a big tumbled stone, a crystal point) and when you compare against a reference object of similar size at the same room temperature. A small warm bead is not automatically fake; it may just be small.

Crystal identification tests combine practical mineralogy with marketplace awareness. Home tests help you spot common fakes, but for valuable specimens, a gemological lab report is the gold standard. Crystal meanings and energetic properties are separate from authenticity — a stone can be genuinely natural without being a 'healing' tool.

How to Buy Genuine Crystals (Without Getting Fooled)

If the tests above tell you how to read a stone, this section tells you how to read a seller. Most fakes enter the market not through elaborate deception but through vague listings, missing disclosures, and prices that quietly assume the buyer will not ask. A reputable seller makes those questions easy to answer — and the five signs below are the ones that consistently separate sellers who know their stock from those who do not.

Five Signs of a Reputable Seller

  • Naming transparency. The listing names the actual mineral species — "Natural Amethyst (SiO₂)," "Clear Quartz," "Lapis Lazuli" — not vague marketing terms like "purple crystal," "energy stone," or "crystal quartz" (which, as we saw, often means glass). Treatments are disclosed: heat-treated citrine, dyed howlite, lab-created quartz, stabilized turquoise. A seller who names stones correctly is signaling that they know the difference and are willing to be specific.
  • Locality disclosure. The listing states a specific origin — Brazil for amethyst, Afghanistan for lapis, the Czech Republic for moldavite, Iran or the American Southwest for turquoise. "Imported" or no locality listed is not proof of a fake, but it is a missed signal. Sellers who know their sources tend to name them, because locality affects both value and care.
  • Mineral data availability. The seller provides the Mohs hardness, specific gravity, or refractive index — or at least links to it. This is a strong signal, because only sellers who understand mineralogy bother. It also lets you cross-check the home tests: if a listing claims a stone is "natural clear quartz" but the density feels wrong, the data gives you a reference point.
  • Clear return policy. A 30-day no-questions-asked return policy is standard among reputable sellers — and it shifts risk away from the buyer. Sellers who move fakes tend to resist returns, because returned fakes expose them. A generous return policy is not proof of authenticity on its own, but its absence is a warning, and its presence gives you time to run the tests at home and send the stone back if it fails.
  • Real photographs. The listing uses actual photos of the specific piece you will receive — not identical stock shots across hundreds of listings. Natural stones vary: each amethyst point has its own zoning, each turquoise bead its own matrix, each lapis chunk its own pyrite pattern. Mass-produced fakes, by contrast, look identical from listing to listing. Real photos that show natural variation between pieces are a positive sign; suspiciously uniform catalog shots are not.

How Earthward Approaches This

At Earthward, we name each stone by its mineral species, state the origin where we know it, and let the stones speak for themselves — natural variation, inclusions, and all. We do not promise perfection, and we do not hide behind vague labels. If a piece is heat-treated, stabilized, or lab-created, we say so. That is what "return to what's real" means to us: not a guarantee of perfection, but a commitment to telling you what you are actually holding.
